FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data with 543 participants assessed at year 2 (ages 11-13) and year 4 (ages 13-15 follow-ups)

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures video game addiction (VGA) via 6-item questionnaire adapted from Bergen Facebook Addiction Scale and social media addiction (SMA) via 6-item questionnaire; both are specific smartphone/social media variables

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear mixed models (LMM) with interaction effects: VGA × Time showed significant effects on loss-related neural activity in nucleus accumbens, bilateral insula, and right ACC with beta coefficients and p-values reported

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple beta coefficients reported: left nucleus accumbens (b=0.106, SE=0.034, 95% CI [0.040, 0.172], p=.002); right insula (b=0.111, SE=0.034, 95% CI [0.045, 0.177], p=.001); right ACC (b=0.111, SE=0.034, 95% CI [0.048, 0.180], p=.001)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis examining changes in reward/loss processing during MID task across two time points in relation to VGA and SMA

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Computers in Human Behavior, 2025

FT_EC7_reason: Smartphone/social media addiction is not the dependent variable in all analyses; paper examines how VGA/SMA predict neural loss sensitivity outcomes. Additionally, paper reports bidirectional associations testing whether neural changes predict subsequent VGA, indicating complex predictor-outcome relationships beyond demographic-only predictors.
notes: Paper distinguishes VGA and SMA separately; findings show bidirectional relationship between VGA and neural loss sensitivity but no comparable long-term effects for SMA. Uses linear mixed models with appropriate statistical corrections (Benjamini-Hochberg).

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study Release 5.1 data with 9,054 youth at 2-year follow-up and 4,007 at 4-year follow-up; cross-lagged analyses include 3,954 participants

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures problematic screen use (continuous measure of screen-related functional impairment from VGAQ, SMAQ, MPIQ questionnaires), self-reported screen time, and phone-checking frequency; includes video game addiction, social media addiction, and mobile phone involvement as specific screen modalities

FT_IC3.evidence: Network analysis using Gaussian graphical models and cross-lagged panel network (CLPN) analyses testing associations between screen use nodes and psychopathology/behavioral/environmental factor nodes; reports edge strengths, centrality measures, and statistical tests across networks

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports edge strength differences with confidence intervals (e.g., problematic mobile phone use A3: 1.20 vs. 0.89, 95% CI = [0.174, 0.366] for females T2); bridge strength differences reported in Figure 2D-E with 95% CI values

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis including cross-sectional networks at T2 and T4 and cross-lagged panel network analyses examining temporal relationships and centrality measures

FT_IC6.evidence: Published full-length report in Journal of Behavioral Addictions, 2025

FT_EC7_reason: Screen use measures serve as both predictors and outcomes in the network model; paper includes cross-sectional analyses and reports screen use predicting psychopathology/behavioral outcomes and environmental factors predicting screen use. Multiple analytical directions prevent FT_EC7 application.
notes: Paper treats problematic screen use as continuous measure capturing functional impairment rather than simple screen time. Network analysis identifies problematic use as more central than screen time measures. Cross-sectional and longitudinal components both present.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data with subsample of 3,687 early adolescents (49.2% female, mean age 11.96 years) assessed with Fitbit at year 2 and answering screen usage questions at year 3 follow-up

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures mature screen usage: R-rated movies and mature-rated video games as specific content categories on screens; participants reported frequency of playing mature-rated video games and watching R-rated movies at year 3

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports multiple inferential tests linking sleep patterns and sensation-seeking to mature screen usage: correlation/SEM analyses with reported coefficients (e.g., sensation-seeking associated with R-rated movie watching 1 year later), t-tests on sleep duration comparing video game/movie watchers

FT_IC4.evidence: Beta coefficients with standard errors and confidence intervals reported in Table 2 (e.g., Sensation seeking score year 2: β(SE) = 0.02(0.02), CI of β = [−0.02, 0.05]; Sleep duration year 2: β(SE) = −0.06(0.02), CI of β = [−0.09, −0.02])

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective longitudinal analysis examining associations between baseline sleep measures/sensation-seeking traits at year 2 and mature screen usage outcomes at year 3

FT_IC6.evidence: Published Original Article in Sleep (Oxford University Press), 2025

FT_EC7_reason: Mature screen usage is the dependent variable in analyses, but predictors include sensation-seeking (individual psychological trait) and sleep characteristics, which are individual-level variables beyond demographic predictors. Additionally, secondary analyses examine bidirectional associations between sleep patterns and mature screen usage.
notes: Uses objective sleep measurement via Fitbit wearables (up to 21 nights at year 2) paired with self-reported mature screen content usage. Prospective design with secondary bidirectional analyses. Sensation-seeking measured via UPPS-P Impulsive Behavior Scale.
